era_context: Late antiquity: Western Roman Empire fragmenting under Germanic pressure; Eastern Roman Empire under Leo I maintains institutional strength. Germanic kingdoms (Ostrogothic, Visigothic, Vandal, Burgundian) control former Roman territories. In China, Northern Wei consolidates northern control; Liu Song declines in the south. Christianity dominates the Mediterranean; Buddhism expands in East Asia. This is a period of political realignment and the consolidation of Germanic kingdoms.

events.title: Euric becomes King of the Visigoths
events.description: Euric became King of the Visigoths in 466 CE, succeeding his brother Ricimer (not to be confused with the Western Roman general Ricimer). Euric (r. 466–484 CE) was one of the most capable Visigothic kings, and his reign marked the height of Visigothic power in the 5th century. He expanded Visigothic territory in Hispania and southern Gaul, consolidated the kingdom's administrative structures, and maintained Roman law codes. Euric's reign saw the Visigothic Kingdom become one of the most stable and prosperous of the Germanic successor states.
key_figures: Euric (King of the Visigoths, r. 466–484 CE)

sources.name: Isidore of Seville, History of the Goths

events.certainty_note: Euric's accession is attested in later sources. The date 466 CE is traditional; some sources place it slightly earlier or later.

events.title: Eastern Roman Empire under Leo I maintains military strength
events.description: Emperor Leo I of the Eastern Roman Empire (r. 457–474 CE) was in the middle of his reign, maintaining the institutional strength and military capability of the Eastern Empire. Leo I was engaged in military campaigns against the Isaurians, the Ostrogoths, and the Vandals. He was also attempting to maintain religious orthodoxy against Monophysitism. By 466 CE, Leo I was consolidating his power and preparing for the major naval expedition against the Vandals that would be launched in 468 CE.
key_figures: Leo I (Eastern Roman Emperor, r. 457–474 CE)
events.certainty_note: Leo I's reign and military activities are well-attested in contemporary sources.

events.title: Northern Wei Dynasty consolidates under Empress Dowager Feng
events.description: The Northern Wei Dynasty was consolidating its control under the regency of Empress Dowager Feng. By 466 CE, the dynasty had achieved stability and was at the height of its power. The dynasty controlled the Yellow River basin and much of northern China. Empress Dowager Feng's capable administration ensured the dynasty's continued strength. The period saw significant cultural synthesis between Xianbei and Han Chinese traditions, with Buddhism serving as a unifying force.
events.certainty_note: Northern Wei's consolidation under Empress Dowager Feng is well-attested in Northern Wei sources.

events.title: Liu Song Dynasty continues internal decline
events.description: The Liu Song Dynasty was in a period of internal decline and fragmentation. Emperor Qianfei (r. 465–472 CE) had recently come to power and was already showing signs of the cruelty and incompetence that would characterize his reign. The court was riven by factional conflict, and regional warlords were increasingly independent. The dynasty's military capacity was declining, and its territorial control was fragmenting. This period of chaos would continue until the dynasty's collapse in 479 CE.
events.certainty_note: Liu Song's internal decline is well-documented in contemporary Chinese sources.

disconfirming_evidence: Euric's accession date is sometimes given as 466 CE, but some sources place it slightly earlier (464 CE) or later (468 CE). The traditional date of 466 CE is widely accepted but not universally certain. The narrative of Visigothic 'consolidation' may overstate the kingdom's stability; it was still decentralized and prone to internal conflict. Leo I's military strength is sometimes overstated; the Eastern Empire faced significant challenges and the 468 CE expedition would prove disastrous.
historiographic_note: Sources for 466 CE are moderate in richness for the Mediterranean and China, but sparse for other regions. Euric's accession is attested in later sources but not in contemporary documentation. Leo I's reign is well-documented in Constantinople-based sources. The Western Roman Empire's collapse is narrated through the lens of Constantinople-based historians and later compilations. Germanic kingdoms are often described through the eyes of their Roman enemies. African, Northern European, and Central Asian history is severely underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Euric's accession in 466 CE led to Visigothic expansion in 467 CE
Leo I's military preparations in 466 CE led to the naval expedition against Vandals in 468 CE
